original_abstract: This article discusses various conceptualizations of illiberalism and adopts a definition that equates the concept with the negation of three liberal democratic principles: limited power, a neutral state, and an open society. The second part of the article explores the implications of this definitional strategy for empirical research, describes the relationship between populism, authoritarianism, and illiberalism, and identifies nine distinct routes to illiberalism: authoritarian, traditionalist, religious, libertarian, nativist-nationalist, populist, paternalist, materialist-technocratic, and left-wing.
humanized_abstract: Conceptual approaches to illiberalism and the way they have come to be used in the scholarly literature are presented. An approach to the concept of illiberalism as the negation of the three fundamental principles of liberal democracy, namely the principle of the restriction of state power, the principle of the neutrality of the state and the principle of an open society is applied in the article. The methodology applied in the article and the consequent research possibilities are detailed in its second part. The interdependencies between populism, authoritarianism and illiberalism are analyzed. Furthermore, the possible ways to illiberalism are identified (1) authoritarian; (2) traditionalist; (3) religious; (4) libertarian; (5) nativist-nationalist; (6) populist; (7) paternalist; (8) materialist-technocratic; and (9) left-wing.
